conflict: Doug's inability to produce comedy on demand — the gap between what the world wants from him and what he is
consequence: Simone identifies what makes Doug genuinely funny — his literalness, his specificity, his resistance to performance — but Doug doesn't trust it, wanting material rather than himself
must_land_beats: Simone's first question ('Tell me something funny') and Doug's forty-five-second silence
The pool-and-roses claim story — Doug tells it as a genuine coverage puzzle, Simone laughs, Doug says 'That's not a joke, that's a coverage gap'
Simone's diagnostic realization: Doug's comedy is in the precision, in the gap between bureaucratic framing and human mess
Doug's conviction that funny means performing — and his lifelong inability to perform
pressure_source: Doug's inability to produce comedy on demand — the gap between what the world wants from him and what he is
